role_summary: SNCC field secretary; treasurer and SNCC representative of the Baltimore Civic Interest Group (CIG); attended the July 1961 founding SNCC coordinating committee meeting in Louisville as CIG's representative; fall 1961 voter-registration organizer in McComb, Mississippi alongside Bob Moses, helping establish the voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket; 1963 organizer in Cambridge, Maryland alongside Gloria Richardson.
ai_reading: Robinson's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a high cosine count of 336, the catalog's clearest single-organizer-pairing within the early SNCC-Mississippi cohort: Robinson's fall 1961 McComb work and McLaurin's 1962-1968 Sunflower County work both fall under the McComb-and-Mississippi field-secretary tradition, and the embedding catches that shared register as a stronger signal than either's pairing with the SNCC headquarters or with later post-1964 Freedom Summer voices. The novel observation his page surfaces is the cross-state-organizer signature: Robinson worked Baltimore, then Mississippi, then Cambridge Maryland in a three-region arc that the historiography often subordinates to single-region narratives; the embedding catches the field-secretary-traveling-organizer vocabulary as a coherent signal regardless of which state he was in at any given moment.
biographical_paragraph: While Robinson's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), his external biographical record is documented through the SNCC Digital Gateway and the institutional records of the Baltimore Civic Interest Group [src: 1, src: 2]. Robinson was born October 2, 1939 and raised in Baltimore, Maryland. He joined the Baltimore Civic Interest Group at the insistence of City Councilman Walter Dixon, became the group's treasurer, and represented CIG at the July 1961 SNCC coordinating committee meeting in Louisville, Kentucky [src: 2]. In fall 1961 he traveled to McComb, Mississippi to work voter registration alongside Bob Moses, helping establish the voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket; he left Mississippi later that fall after white violence escalated, including the murder of fellow organizer Herbert Lee [src: 2]. He worked the desegregation campaigns along Route 40 between Washington, D.C. and New York City in the early 1960s and joined the 1963 Cambridge, Maryland organizing alongside Gloria Richardson [src: 2]. CIG registered nearly 10,000 Black voters in Baltimore during his tenure; he mentored Northern-college SNCC recruits including future Eyes on the Prize producer Judy Richardson (catalog entry 69, the same Robinson-mentee whose own page documents the Atlanta-and-Mississippi arc that followed) [src: 2].

sources.title: Reginald Robinson oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
